description: This test case evaluates the system's ability to retrieve and synthesize information from multiple, separate document sections to answer a complex question.
question: How do grain size and interfacial properties of copper vias influence via extrusion during thermal cycling in 3D integration, and what mechanisms are proposed to mitigate via extrusion based on the material and structural properties of the vias?

gold_context_sources.evidence: Via extrusion has been a major concern for the yield and reliability of 3D integration. It manifests the non-recoverable plastic deformation near the top of the via during thermal cycles. In a recent study, via extrusion was observed with atomic force microscopy and was found to increase with maximum temperature in thermal cycles. The rough surface profile suggested microscopically stochastic behavior, which was found to follow the grain structure near the surface of the via, possibly due to grain boundary sliding. Two types of vias with different average grain sizes (2.83 µm for small grain (SG) TSVs versus 3.82 µm for large grain (LG) TSVs) were studied. Nanoindentation measurements showed a yield strength of 250 MPa for SG TSVs and 190 MPa for LG TSVs, consistent with the Hall–Petch relation. The average via extrusion was 117 nm for SG TSVs and 147 nm for LG TSVs, indicating a correlation between the average grain size and the amount of via extrusion. The smallest 20% via extrusion was dominated by (111) grains with large grain size, while the largest 20% extrusion was dominated by (200) and (110) grains with small grain size. For vias containing three or more grains at the top surface, extrusion was about twice that of vias containing only one large grain. The results suggest that a single grain at the top of the via would be effective in mitigating via extrusion, which was found to reduce the via extrusion substantially. Interfacial sliding and Cu plasticity have been suggested as two possible mechanisms for via extrusion. Assuming a perfectly bonded interface between the Cu via and Si, the residual extrusion at room temperature was significantly reduced, by ~3x. Using a cohesive model for the via/Si interface, via extrusions at room and maximum temperatures were found to be bound by two limits, frictionless sliding as the upper bound and a perfectly bonded interface (no sliding) as the lower bound. Hence, via extrusion can be suppressed by improving the interfacial adhesion to resist sliding.

A simple analytical model was formulated by assuming perfect plasticity in Cu and frictionless interfacial sliding. When the TSV is subjected to a thermal cycle from room temperature to a high process temperature and back, the mismatch of thermal expansion between the Cu via and Si induces a compressive stress in Cu upon heating. Plastic yielding of Cu is predicted when heated above a critical temperature. Beyond this critical temperature, the Cu via deforms plastically, leading to more via extrusion at higher temperatures. The plastic extrusion does not vanish after cooling, resulting in a non-zero residual extrusion after a full thermal cycle. The magnitude of the residual extrusion depends on the highest temperature during thermal cycling and the plastic yield strength of the Cu via. Increasing the yield strength of Cu increases the yield temperature and thus decreases the residual extrusion for the same thermal load. This model is qualitatively consistent with experimental measurements and finite element analysis (FEA) simulations, which show higher residual extrusion for large grain vias than for small grain vias under the same thermal load. Effects of interfacial properties and via dimensions on extrusion have been studied by numerical simulations. The residual extrusion is significantly reduced by assuming a perfectly bonded interface. Via extrusion can be suppressed by improving interfacial adhesion to resist sliding. The results show a general trend of decreasing extrusion with smaller via diameter and depth, but it is unclear if reliability improves with smaller diameter due to the statistics of largest extrusion events.

ground_truth_answer:
The grain size and interfacial properties of copper vias critically influence via extrusion during thermal cycling in 3D integration. Smaller grain size vias (SG TSVs, average grain size ~2.83 µm) exhibit higher yield strength (250 MPa) compared to larger grain size vias (LG TSVs, ~3.82 µm, 190 MPa), consistent with the Hall–Petch relation. This higher yield strength in SG TSVs leads to lower average via extrusion (117 nm) compared to LG TSVs (147 nm). The stochastic nature of via extrusion correlates with grain structure at the top of the via: vias with single large grains, especially (111) orientation, show significantly reduced extrusion, while those with multiple smaller grains and orientations like (200) and (110) exhibit larger extrusion. This suggests that fabricating vias with uniform single grains at the top surface can effectively mitigate extrusion.

Interfacial properties also play a vital role. Models and experiments show that perfectly bonded interfaces between Cu vias and silicon substrate reduce residual extrusion by approximately three times compared to frictionless sliding interfaces. Via extrusion can thus be suppressed by improving interfacial adhesion to resist sliding. The plastic deformation of copper vias during thermal cycling is influenced by the mismatch in thermal expansion between copper and silicon, which induces compressive stress leading to plastic yielding beyond a critical temperature. The residual extrusion remains after cooling due to plastic deformation.

To mitigate via extrusion, strategies include increasing the yield strength of copper vias (e.g., by controlling grain size), improving interfacial bonding to prevent sliding, and optimizing via dimensions. Although smaller vias tend to have lower average extrusion, the overall reliability depends on the distribution of maximum extrusion events, which may not improve simply by reducing via size. Therefore, material engineering of grain structure and interfacial adhesion are key mechanisms proposed to control and reduce via extrusion, enhancing yield and reliability in 3D integrated circuits.
